text: Hey everybody, welcome to This is My Architecture. My name is Peter. I'm here with Siba from
Publicis Sapient. Good to have you here. Thank you, Peter. All right Siba, so before we talk
about the board, what do you guys do? So Publicis Sapient has been helping our clients in all
things digital for over two decades. We help our clients in their journey of digital business
transformation. All right, so I know you've built this pretty cool account generation engine,
right? So tell me what the reason was to build this and talk me through it works. So technology
is changing very fast. In our journey to help our clients in their digital business transformation
story, we need our engineers to be equipped with newer technologies, tools, and solutions. So we
need our engineers to be learning and experimenting on those and we need a platform for that. All
right, so we're going to provide dedicated accounts to each of your engineers to test things out,
right? So how does this all work? Show me. So the account creation process is fairly simple.
We kept it very straightforward. We have a user portal through which the user summits an account
provisioning request. The API Gateway listens to the request and forwards it to a validate Lambda
function. The validate Lambda function actually verifies a few information like email, domain, so that
the request is coming from within the organization. Once it does that, it sends a notification to the user
acknowledging that the request has been received and then forwards it to create Lambda function. The
create function actually creates and provisions the account. Okay, understood. So first and foremost, the validation part is interesting. You make sure that everyone is allowed to create those

accounts. They're being well integrated with API Gateway, kind of like a first-class citizen in here. And you use SQS to continue the flow. Now the creation is interesting. What's happening right here?
So creation is where actually the account is provisioned. In the create process, we identify the master account. We use a multi-master

account strategy. We identify the master account strategy. We identify the least algorithm, least use algorithm to find the master account in which the child account needs to be created. We configure the account. We assign the account an organization unit and create budget alerts. And finally send a notification to the user and share the credentials.

All right. So there's two things. Actually, three things that I'm interested in. First of all, the government of those accounts. You see organizations place a role in here. And then we have the budget control. So what happens on the organization side?

So what we have taken advantage of? So what we have taken advantage of? So what we have taken advantage of our nations is that we have an active account and we have an inactive OU. We have active OU and inactive OU. Active OU is where all the accounts are hosted. And all the policies on security and cost are enforced on active OU. Inactive OU is essentially a deny all service. When an account is not in use, it is in a deny OU.
All right. Okay. So first we've looked around using IAM policies to manage the whole governance. But we were lucky that SCP was launched around the same time.
Sure. And, you know, we took advantage of it so that we can centrally control and manage all the governance.

Perfect. Perfect. Okay. So you were able to say which services are made available to your users eventually. One thing we have not talked about yet is the confirmation part. I assume it's part of the kind of blueprinting, right?
Yeah. So the create process actually uses CloudFormation to provision the account and create budget alerts and all of that. Within the SCP, we have restricted the services that user can use.
Yeah.
We have also limited the kind of instance types that user can use. For example, reserved instances are forbidden. We have also restricted marketplace AMIs, right?
So all of this is done within the SCP.
Right here. Okay. Okay. Perfect. So then the last part I've asked already, which is the budget control, right? At some stage, you probably want to, you know, limit the usage of service.
Yeah.
So what's going on here?
So we take advantage, we use budget alarms and SES to kind of manage the, monitor the budget. We have configured alerts to at 50%, 70%, and 90% of usage.
Mm-hmm.
And once the user is continuously notified. Once the user gets to 100% budget usage, his account is actually moved to the inactive OU that I referred earlier, where all services are denied.
And, you know, after a certain hours, you know, we completely nuke the account so that, you know, we don't end up consuming the budget.
All right. Okay. Perfect. So we remove those accounts that are not in use anymore.
Yes.
